# Related Work {#sec-related-work}

MaintAlign sits at the intersection of two research areas that are usually studied separately: maintenance optimization and production scheduling. This chapter surveys how existing work addresses each of these areas and, more importantly, what happens when researchers try to combine them. The review is organized around three themes that map directly onto MaintAlign's architecture: optimization methods for scheduling problems, simulation-based approaches for validating those solutions, and the growing push to integrate maintenance decisions with production planning.

## Scheduling Optimization in Manufacturing {#sec-scheduling-optimization}

At its core, MaintAlign needs to solve a combinatorial optimization problem: given a set of machines that need maintenance at different times, find a schedule that minimizes total cost while respecting constraints like production windows and crew availability. This type of problem has a long history in operations research, and it shows up in many forms across manufacturing.

The most common family of solution methods in the literature is metaheuristic algorithms. These are general-purpose search strategies that explore large solution spaces without guaranteeing they will find the absolute best answer, but they tend to find good answers quickly. Genetic algorithms (GAs) are probably the most popular choice. @Lunardi2018 compare a GA against a discrete firefly algorithm for the flexible job-shop scheduling problem, where each operation can run on multiple machines. Their experiments show both methods producing high-quality schedules, with the firefly algorithm edging out the GA on some instances. @Zhao2019 also use a GA, but in a maintenance-specific context: they solve a selective maintenance problem for multi-state systems, where only some components can be maintained during a limited break between missions. Their key contribution is incorporating Functionally Significant Items --- components ranked by safety importance using the Analytic Hierarchy Process --- into the maintenance decision. This matters because in real plants, not every component is equally critical, and a pure cost-optimization approach might skip safety-critical items.

The downside of metaheuristics is that they require careful tuning. The population size, crossover rate, mutation rate, and termination criteria all affect solution quality, and what works well on one problem instance might not transfer to another. @Zhang2025 tackle this by designing an improved Harris eagle optimization algorithm for their integrated preventive maintenance model. Their objective function rolls together maintenance costs, defective product costs, shutdown costs, and tardiness penalties into a single expression. The Harris eagle algorithm handles this composite objective, but the authors note that tuning the algorithm's parameters was itself a nontrivial task.

Another limitation of the metaheuristic literature is that feasibility is often treated differently than in constraint-based models. In many GA-style approaches, the algorithm explores candidate schedules first and then relies on penalty terms or repair procedures to push bad solutions back toward feasibility. That can work well, but it also means that part of the search effort is spent generating schedules that violate important operational rules before those violations are corrected. In a maintenance setting, this distinction matters. A schedule that assigns two tasks to the same crew at the same time, ignores a production blackout period, or forgets a dependency between upstream and downstream machines is not just slightly suboptimal; it may be operationally unusable. This is one reason MaintAlign does not treat metaheuristics as the default choice. The issue is not that GA-based methods are weak — the literature shows that they can be highly effective — but that their strengths are often greatest in settings where solution flexibility matters more than strict rule satisfaction. MaintAlign, by contrast, is aimed at a setting where feasibility itself is a major part of the problem, because maintenance windows, labor limits, and production dependencies are not optional details added after the fact. They are central pieces of the scheduling decision from the beginning.

## Constraint Programming as an Alternative {#sec-constraint-programming}

Constraint programming (CP) takes a fundamentally different approach. Instead of searching stochastically like a GA, CP solvers systematically explore the solution space by propagating constraints to prune infeasible regions before they are ever evaluated. This makes CP particularly effective when the problem has many hard constraints --- things that absolutely must be satisfied, not just soft preferences.

@Winter2021 demonstrate this in the automotive supply industry, where paint shop scheduling involves a web of constraints: color sequencing rules, drying time requirements, batch size limits, and due dates. They develop CP models that produce feasible schedules for real factory instances, and they prove that the decision variant of the problem is NP-complete. The practical takeaway is that despite the theoretical hardness, CP finds good schedules where manual planning by full-time employees previously took much longer.

The question of how CP compares to other methods is addressed directly by @Goponenko2024, who benchmark IBM's CP Optimizer against backfilling heuristics for HPC cluster scheduling. Their results show that CP improves packing efficiency in most cases, though it still has scalability issues with very large wait queues. For MaintAlign, this is an encouraging finding: maintenance scheduling problems tend to be moderate in size (tens to hundreds of components, not thousands of jobs), which is exactly the regime where CP performs well.

Perhaps the most relevant work for MaintAlign's solver choice is @Lorenzi2025, who combine CP-SAT --- the same solver MaintAlign uses --- with a genetic algorithm for the job-shop scheduling problem. Their hybrid approach (HCPGA) uses CP-SAT to generate feasible solutions that seed the GA's initial population, then lets the GA refine from there. On 74 benchmark instances, standalone CP-SAT and HCPGA achieve comparable makespan results, but the hybrid method uses significantly less time and memory. This suggests that CP-SAT is already competitive on its own for moderate-size scheduling problems, and that hybridization is mainly beneficial when scaling to very large instances. MaintAlign currently uses CP-SAT as a standalone solver, but these results point toward a natural extension if scaling becomes necessary.

## Simulation for Validation and Optimization {#sec-simulation-validation}

Finding a mathematically optimal schedule is only useful if it actually performs well under real-world uncertainty. Machines do not fail on a predictable timetable --- they follow probability distributions. A schedule that looks perfect on paper might fall apart when a machine breaks down two days earlier than expected. This is why simulation plays a central role in validating maintenance optimization systems.

@Gutschi2024 make this point forcefully. They present a procedural model for evaluating predictive maintenance systems through discrete-event simulation, and their key finding is sobering: a predictive maintenance model with poor precision can actually make things *worse* than having no predictive system at all. False alarms trigger unnecessary maintenance that disrupts production, while missed alarms lead to the same surprise breakdowns that preventive maintenance was supposed to prevent. Their procedure explicitly accounts for this imperfection, quantifying the net economic impact of both types of errors. For MaintAlign, this underscores the importance of testing the optimizer's schedules against realistic failure scenarios rather than just reporting the objective function value.

@Klanke2021 take a different angle on simulation by using it as the optimization engine itself. They pair a GA with an industrial-strength discrete-event simulator for a large-scale formulation plant. The simulator serves as the fitness evaluator: each candidate schedule from the GA is run through a detailed plant model (down to operator shift patterns and mass balances) to compute its actual performance. This simulation-based optimization approach ensures that the computed schedules are directly applicable to the real plant, not just theoretically good. The tradeoff is computational cost --- each fitness evaluation requires a full simulation run.

@Krause2020 extends simulation-based scheduling to manufacturing networks rather than single plants. Using an actor-critic architecture combined with a GA, they optimize schedules for over 375 product types across 180+ manufacturing resources. Their work highlights that in complex manufacturing environments with global supply chains, the gap between what an ERP system can handle and what a proper optimization approach can deliver is substantial. MaintAlign takes a similar philosophy: it uses Monte Carlo simulation to validate schedules by generating thousands of random failure scenarios from Weibull distributions and comparing the optimizer's output against simpler baselines.

@Santos2019 provide another perspective on simulation for maintenance by modeling multi-unit systems with generalized stochastic Petri nets coupled with Monte Carlo simulation. Their setup uses Weibull distributions with increasing failure rates for component degradation --- the same distributional assumption that MaintAlign makes --- and they compare four maintenance strategies: basic corrective, basic preventive, and both of those combined with opportunistic maintenance. Their finding that opportunistic maintenance strategies consistently reduce total costs relative to non-opportunistic ones reinforces a core design choice in MaintAlign, which groups nearby maintenance activities to save setup costs. The Petri net formalism is different from MaintAlign's constraint programming approach, but the underlying modeling assumptions (Weibull failures, multiple interacting components, cost minimization under uncertainty) are closely aligned.

@Cacereno2023 push the simulation-optimization coupling further by using multi-objective evolutionary algorithms together with discrete event simulation to simultaneously optimize both system design (how many redundant components to include) and preventive maintenance intervals. Their case study on a nuclear power plant safety system treats availability and operational cost as competing objectives, which means there is no single best answer --- instead, the optimizer produces a set of Pareto-optimal tradeoffs. This multi-objective framing is worth noting because MaintAlign currently optimizes a single composite cost objective, but the tradeoff between cost and reliability that @Cacereno2023 make explicit is implicitly present in MaintAlign's formulation too: choosing a longer maintenance interval saves direct cost but increases failure risk. Their work also confirms that the computational burden of simulation-based fitness evaluation is real --- each candidate solution requires a full simulation run, and population-based algorithms need many such evaluations. MaintAlign sidesteps this issue by separating the optimization step (solved by CP-SAT using precomputed cost tables) from the validation step (Monte Carlo simulation applied only to the final schedule), which keeps the computational cost manageable.

To make the Weibull distribution concrete: it describes the probability that a component fails before time $t$ using two parameters, a shape parameter $\beta$ and a scale parameter $\eta$. The cumulative distribution function is $F(t) = 1 - e^{-(t/\eta)^\beta}$. When $\beta > 1$, the failure rate increases with age, which is the wear-out behavior that makes preventive maintenance worthwhile. For instance, a pump bearing with $\beta = 2.0$ and $\eta = 4000$ hours has about a 6% chance of failing before 1000 hours, but a 39% chance of failing before 3000 hours. That accelerating risk is exactly what MaintAlign's cost tables capture when deciding when to schedule maintenance.

@fig-weibull-curves illustrates how the shape parameter $\beta$ changes the character of the failure process. The left panel shows the cumulative failure probability over time; the right panel shows the instantaneous failure rate (hazard function), $h(t) = \frac{\beta}{\eta}\left(\frac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta - 1}$. When $\beta < 1$, the hazard rate actually *decreases* with age --- this is the "infant mortality" pattern where early failures are common but survivors become more reliable. At $\beta = 1$, failures arrive at a constant rate regardless of age, meaning preventive replacement offers no benefit over running until breakdown. The cases that matter most for MaintAlign are $\beta > 1$, where the hazard rate climbs over time. A component with $\beta = 3.0$ shows a sharply accelerating risk curve: nearly flat for the first couple thousand hours, then rising steeply. That steep rise is exactly the window where scheduling preventive maintenance pays off, because the expected cost of waiting another week grows rapidly as the component ages. The flatter curve at $\beta = 1.5$ still increases, but more gradually, meaning the scheduling optimizer has more flexibility about when to intervene. These differences in curve shape are what drive MaintAlign's cost tables to recommend different maintenance intervals for different components, even when those components have the same scale parameter $\eta$.

![Weibull cumulative distribution function (left) and hazard function (right) for several values of the shape parameter $\beta$, all with scale parameter $\eta = 4000$ hours. Values of $\beta > 1$ produce the increasing failure rates that justify preventive maintenance.](images/weibull_curves.png){#fig-weibull-curves}

## Bridging Maintenance and Production {#sec-maintenance-production}

A recurring problem in manufacturing is that maintenance and production planning happen in separate silos. The production team wants maximum uptime; the maintenance team wants to service machines before they fail. When these two groups do not coordinate, the result is either unnecessary downtime (maintenance scheduled during peak production) or deferred maintenance that leads to breakdowns.

@Zhang2025 address this coupling head-on by modeling how equipment deterioration affects product quality. Their integrated optimization considers maintenance costs, defective product costs, shutdown costs, and production delay penalties simultaneously. The insight is that ignoring quality degradation leads to schedules that look cheaper on paper but actually cost more once scrap and rework are factored in. For example, a machine running past its optimal maintenance window might still be "working" in the sense that it has not broken down, but the parts it produces may increasingly fail quality checks.

@Mistarihi2025 push the integration further by proposing a reinforcement learning framework that unifies production scheduling and maintenance planning in Industry 5.0 environments. Their approach dynamically adjusts both scheduling and maintenance decisions based on continuous feedback from the production system. While MaintAlign does not use reinforcement learning --- it relies on constraint programming with precomputed cost tables --- the motivation is the same: maintenance and production decisions are coupled and should be optimized together, not sequentially.

MaintAlign handles this coupling through its production chain dependency model. When machine A feeds parts to machine B, taking A down for maintenance starves B of inputs. The CP-SAT model captures these dependencies as constraints, ensuring that the solver accounts for downstream production impacts when scheduling maintenance windows. This is a simpler approach than the RL-based framework of @Mistarihi2025, but it has the advantage of producing explainable, deterministic schedules rather than learned policies that may be difficult to audit.

## Positioning MaintAlign {#sec-positioning}

The literature reviewed above reveals a clear landscape. Metaheuristic algorithms like GAs are the dominant approach for manufacturing scheduling and maintenance optimization [@Zhang2025; @Zhao2019; @Lunardi2018; @Klanke2021]. Constraint programming is well-established for scheduling problems with hard constraints [@Winter2021; @Goponenko2024] and has recently been combined with metaheuristics for hybrid approaches [@Lorenzi2025]. Simulation is essential for validating that optimized schedules hold up under uncertainty [@Gutschi2024; @Krause2020; @Santos2019; @Cacereno2023], and there is growing recognition that maintenance and production must be planned jointly rather than independently [@Zhang2025; @Mistarihi2025].

MaintAlign draws on all of these threads but combines them differently than any single existing system. It uses CP-SAT as its core solver rather than a metaheuristic, which gives it the ability to express hard scheduling constraints (production windows, crew limits, dependency chains) declaratively. It models equipment failure using Weibull distributions and accounts for imperfect maintenance through the Kijima Type I virtual age model, where each maintenance action reduces a component's effective age by a fraction $q$ rather than restoring it to brand-new condition. It groups nearby maintenance activities opportunistically to save setup costs. And it validates everything through Monte Carlo simulation, comparing against fixed-interval and run-to-failure baselines.

A second point of distinction is that MaintAlign is designed as a decision-support tool rather than only as an optimization experiment. Many papers in this literature evaluate a method by showing that it improves makespan, cost, or some other objective on benchmark instances, which is important, but that is not the same as building a tool that a planner could realistically inspect and use. MaintAlign therefore gives unusual weight to interpretability. Its use of precomputed Weibull-based cost tables, explicit production-chain constraints, and simulation-based comparison against simple baselines is meant to make the final recommendation easier to explain. A planner can ask why one machine was prioritized over another and trace the answer to a combination of age-dependent failure cost, dependency structure, and crew or timing constraints. That kind of transparency does not eliminate the difficulty of the optimization problem, but it does make the system easier to question, debug, and refine. In that sense, MaintAlign is positioned not only between maintenance optimization and production scheduling, but also between theory and practice: it aims to preserve the rigor of operations research while still producing outputs that a human decision-maker can interpret and challenge.

No single paper in this review combines all of these elements. The closest are @Zhang2025, who integrate maintenance with production quality but use a metaheuristic rather than CP, and @Lorenzi2025, who use CP-SAT but for job-shop scheduling rather than maintenance. MaintAlign occupies the gap between these two lines of work: it brings the constraint programming approach that has proven effective for scheduling into the maintenance optimization domain, where metaheuristics currently dominate.
